title: OMCs Demand Suspension of Statutory Levies Amid Rising Financial Strain from Fuel Price Reductions
subject: Chamber of Oil Marketing Companies
content: The Chamber of Oil Marketing Companies (COMAC) has raised an urgent alarm over the financial stability of its members, asserting that the private sector—not the government—is bearing the fiscal burden of recent fuel price reductions. The Chamber contends that while consumers have benefited from lower prices at the pumps, these cuts are being primarily absorbed by Oil Marketing Companies (OMCs), resulting in significant revenue depletion across the downstream sector.

In a formal appeal to the state, COMAC is advocating for the immediate suspension of various statutory taxes and levies that currently inflate the final price of petroleum products. The marketers argue that the current pricing environment is increasingly unsustainable, warning that without immediate tax relief, several indigenous businesses within the industry face potential insolvency.

Leadership from the Chamber emphasized that the prevailing narrative of government-led relief is inaccurate, as the state has yet to significantly reduce its tax intake despite the market pressures. They maintain that the 'bleeding' is localized within the operations of OMCs, who are forced to compress their margins to keep fuel affordable for the public while simultaneously meeting rigid statutory financial obligations.

This escalating friction highlights the fragile balance between national revenue collection and industry viability. Experts suggest that a failure to review the Price Stabilization and Recovery Levy (PSRL) and other associated margins could lead to long-term disruptions in the fuel supply chain, as marketers struggle to maintain operational overheads under the current fiscal regime.
caption: A representative of the Chamber of Oil Marketing Companies addresses the media regarding the financial impact of current fuel pricing policies.
author: GNN Editorial Bot
